In modern times, Cloudflare's public DNS uses 1.1.1.1, and this conflict can cause issues — best practice is to change the virtual IP to something from RFC 5737's TEST-NET space (192.0.2.0/24) or the RFC 6598 shared address space (100.64.0.0/10) to avoid any real-world collision.

Catalyst 9800 difference: The modern Catalyst 9800 WLC (IOS-XE) doesn't use a standalone 'virtual interface' in the same way. Web-auth configuration moves into the parameter-map for the WLAN, and the virtual interface concept is largely abstracted away. For the CCNA, focus on AireOS terminology.

Exam note: 'Which WLC interface is used for web auth redirect?' → virtual. 'What's the default IP of the WLC virtual interface?' → 1.1.1.1. Low-frequency but well-known question.

hack.memory: Virtual interface = WLC's decoy address (1.1.1.1 by default). Never a real endpoint on the wire — just a placeholder for DHCP relay, captive portal redirects, and mobility. 'If the user is redirected to a login page, that page lives on the virtual IP.' Change it to 192.0.2.1 to avoid conflicting with Cloudflare DNS.
hack.practice: On an AireOS WLC: Controller > Interfaces > virtual — note the IP is 1.1.1.1. Create a guest WLAN with Web Auth. Connect a test client and open a browser — the redirect URL will contain 1.1.1.1 as the portal host. This is the virtual interface in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 covers WLC interfaces. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. Exam question: 'default virtual interface IP?' = 1.1.1.1. 'What is it used for?' = DHCP relay, web auth redirect, mobility.

1.1.e.info:
A dynamic interface on an AireOS WLC is a VLAN-mapped interface that carries wireless client traffic onto the wired network. Think of it as the WLC's equivalent of a switch SVI — one per VLAN that wireless clients will land in.

Typical deployment: You have Corporate (VLAN 10), Guest (VLAN 99), and IoT (VLAN 30) wireless networks. On the WLC you create three dynamic interfaces: 'dyn-corp' mapped to VLAN 10 with IP in 10.0.10.x, 'dyn-guest' mapped to VLAN 99 with IP in 10.0.99.x, 'dyn-iot' for VLAN 30. Each WLAN is then bound to the appropriate dynamic interface. When a client connects to 'Corporate' SSID, their traffic exits onto VLAN 10.

Configuration (AireOS): Controller > Interfaces > New > name = 'dyn-corp', VLAN ID = 10, port = (trunk port number), IP, mask, gateway, DHCP server. The WLC must be connected to the wired network via an 802.1Q trunk that carries all these VLANs.

Central switching flow: Client → AP → CAPWAP tunnel → WLC → dynamic interface → wired VLAN. The WLC de-encapsulates the CAPWAP, applies any ACLs, and emits the frame onto the correct VLAN via the dynamic interface.

Catalyst 9800 difference: The 9800 uses traditional IOS-XE VLAN interfaces and policy profiles rather than the AireOS 'dynamic interface' construct. The concept is the same — map wireless clients to VLANs — but the configuration is different. For the CCNA, the AireOS terminology is still what's tested.

Exam hook: 'What WLC interface type maps a WLAN to a wired VLAN?' → dynamic interface. 'Which interface behaves most like a switch SVI?' → dynamic interface.

children.name: Client traffic lands on VLAN 10 wired

hack.memory: Dynamic interface = WLC's SVI. One per user VLAN. Tells the WLC 'wireless clients on THIS WLAN land on THAT VLAN.' Without dynamic interfaces, the WLC doesn't know where to dump decrypted client traffic.
hack.practice: In AireOS: create two WLANs (Corporate, Guest), two dynamic interfaces (one per VLAN), and bind them. Verify with 'show wlan summary' and 'show interface summary'. Connect clients to each SSID and check they land on the correct VLAN (ipconfig should show the right subnet).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 covers dynamic interfaces. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. Exam: know the dynamic interface is the 'per-VLAN' wireless-to-wired mapping.

1.1.e.info:
The service port is the WLC's dedicated out-of-band (OOB) management port — a separate physical Ethernet interface used exclusively for administrative access to the controller. It does NOT carry client data, CAPWAP, or any wireless operational traffic.

Out-of-band means: the service port connects to a separate management network (typically a /30 or small subnet dedicated to device management) isolated from the production network. If the production network melts down and the management interface becomes unreachable, you can still SSH to the WLC via the service port and troubleshoot.

Key limitations:

  • Always in its own VLAN (cannot be tagged/trunked like other interfaces).
  • No routing by default — you need static routes if the admin workstation is not on the same subnet as the service port.
  • Not used for client DHCP, AP CAPWAP, RADIUS, or any wireless service.

Hardware: On physical WLCs (3504, 5520, 9800-L/40/80), the service port is a clearly labeled Ethernet jack separate from the main data ports. Virtual/cloud WLCs may omit it since OOB access is managed by the hypervisor or cloud console.

Exam relevance: Low. Just recognize that 'service port' = out-of-band management ONLY. If an exam question says 'the admin uses a separate management network for emergency access to the WLC' → that's the service port. If it says 'APs form CAPWAP tunnels to...' → that's the management interface, NOT service port.

hack.memory: Service port = the WLC's fire escape. Separate door on a separate network, for when the front door (management interface) is blocked. Admin access only — no wireless traffic. Rarely touched in day-to-day ops, but a lifesaver in a total outage.
hack.practice: On a physical 3504/5520: find the 'Service' port — a physical Ethernet jack separate from the data ports. Assign an IP like 192.168.99.2 on an isolated management VLAN. SSH to that IP from a dedicated jump host. This is OOB management in practice.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. Exam weight is low, but know the service port's role = out-of-band admin only.

1.1.e.info:
Radio Resource Management (RRM) is the Cisco WLC feature set that automatically optimizes the RF environment across all lightweight APs it manages. Without RRM, an administrator would have to manually assign channels and transmit power for every AP — impractical at any real scale. RRM does it continuously and dynamically.

Three core RRM algorithms:

  • DCA (Dynamic Channel Assignment) — picks non-overlapping channels for each AP to minimize co-channel interference. Avoids the 'all APs on channel 6' newbie mistake. Reconsiders channel assignments periodically or when interference changes.
  • TPC (Transmit Power Control) — adjusts each AP's transmit power so neighboring APs just barely hear each other. If all APs transmit at max power, they drown each other out; TPC finds the sweet spot where coverage is good but adjacent cells don't overlap too much.
  • Coverage Hole Detection (CHDM) — detects areas where clients have poor signal and automatically increases nearby AP power to fill the gap, or alerts the admin to add an AP.

How it works: Every AP uses small slices of its data-serving time to scan other channels (off-channel scanning). Measurements — noise floor, neighbor AP signal strengths, rogue AP sightings, interference — are sent to the WLC over CAPWAP. The WLC has a global view and runs RRM algorithms to optimize channels and powers across the entire RF group.

RF Groups: In large deployments, multiple WLCs form an 'RF Group' (shared leader election + combined RRM decisions) so channel/power decisions are optimized globally rather than per-controller.

Exam hook: 'Which WLC feature automatically assigns AP channels to avoid interference?' → RRM / DCA. 'Which feature adjusts AP transmit power?' → RRM / TPC.

states: Send RF data to WLC via CAPWAP

hack.memory: RRM = the WLC's auto-pilot for Wi-Fi. Three jobs: DCA (channels), TPC (power), CHDM (coverage holes). Without RRM, a human would have to walk every AP and spray-paint it with a channel. With RRM, the WLC tunes all 500 APs every few minutes based on live RF data. Set it and forget it.
hack.practice: On a WLC: Wireless > 802.11a/b > RRM > Dynamic Channel Assignment. Note the settings (default interval, sensitivity). View 'show ap auto-rf 802.11a' for an AP — you'll see its current channel, neighbor list, and power level. Change DCA to 'on-demand' and trigger a recalculation. Watch channels shift across APs.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. Exam: expect 'what is RRM?' (auto channel/power) and recognition of DCA and TPC as its sub-features.

1.1.e.info:
Rogue AP detection is the WLC feature that identifies unauthorized access points operating in or near your environment. Rogues are a major wireless security concern: a user plugs in a cheap home router to the corporate wired network, bypassing 802.1X, and creates a back door into the LAN.

How detection works: Lightweight APs — especially in local or monitor mode — continuously scan other channels off-channel. When they hear a beacon or probe response from an AP whose BSSID is not in the managed AP list, they report it to the WLC as a 'suspected rogue.'

Rogue classification levels:

  • Unclassified — just seen in the air, no threat assessment yet.
  • Friendly — manually marked as benign (e.g., a neighboring business's AP).
  • Malicious — suspected bad actor (e.g., matches SSID of your corporate network = possible evil twin).
  • Rogue on wire — the WLC verified the rogue AP's MAC appears on YOUR wired network via RLDP (Rogue Location Discovery Protocol) or switchport tracing. This is the worst case: a rogue AP plugged into corporate LAN.

Rogue Containment (optional): The WLC can direct trusted APs to send deauth frames to clients of the rogue AP, preventing them from associating. This is legally gray — containing APs outside your own premises violates FCC Part 15 rules and has cost some enterprises FCC fines. Use containment carefully and only for rogues that are verifiably on your wire.

Switchport tracing: Advanced WLCs integrate with switches to identify the exact wired port where a rogue AP is plugged in — admins can then disable that port and track down the device.

Exam hook: 'What WLC feature identifies unauthorized APs?' → rogue AP detection. 'What's an evil twin?' → rogue AP broadcasting the same SSID as the legitimate network.

children.name: WLC classifies: Unclassified / Friendly / Malicious / On-Wire

hack.memory: Rogue AP = the wireless equivalent of someone plugging a thumb drive into a corporate PC. LAPs sniff nearby broadcasts; anything not on the managed list gets flagged. 'Evil twin' = rogue pretending to be your SSID to steal creds. Worst case: 'rogue on wire' = that rogue is actually on YOUR corporate LAN. Hunt it down via switchport tracing.
hack.practice: In the WLC GUI: Security > Rogue Policies. Set up a rogue AP test by bringing a cheap home router near your APs. Within minutes, it should appear in Rogues > Detected. Classify it, and (in lab only) try containment to see deauth frames being sent. Never container outside your own premises.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 26. Exam: know rogue AP detection exists, know evil twin (same SSID as legit) is a specific attack. Legal note on containment not on exam but good to know.

1.1.e.info:
Cisco DNA Center (renamed Catalyst Center in 2023) is Cisco's centralized network management and automation platform. It's the centerpiece of Cisco's Intent-Based Networking (IBN) vision: define the business intent once, let the platform translate that into device configurations across switches, routers, and WLCs, then continuously verify the network is delivering on that intent.

Scope: Unlike a WLC (which manages wireless only), DNA Center manages your entire campus network — wired switches, wireless controllers + APs, routers, and SD-WAN fabric. One pane of glass for the whole enterprise.

Runs on: dedicated appliance (DN-HW-APL / DN2-HW-APL server — usually 3-node cluster for HA). Also available as a virtual appliance for smaller deployments.

Key differentiators from traditional management:

  • Intent-based — declare what you want, not line-by-line CLI.
  • Automation engine — pushes configs to thousands of devices simultaneously.
  • Assurance — AI/ML-driven health monitoring, anomaly detection, root cause analysis.
  • API-driven — full REST API for automation integration.
  • Fabric builder for SD-Access (software-defined campus).

Catalyst Center vs Meraki Dashboard: Both are Cisco. Catalyst Center is for Catalyst/traditional hardware with on-prem deployment. Meraki Dashboard is a SaaS cloud-managed platform for Meraki gear. Don't confuse them on the exam — the question 'Cisco's SDN controller for Catalyst' = Catalyst Center / DNA Center.

Exam hook: 'What is Cisco's intent-based networking controller?' → DNA Center / Catalyst Center. 'What is DNA Center's role in wireless?' → manages WLCs as part of broader fabric. 'What replaced Prime Infrastructure?' → DNA Center.

hack.memory: DNA Center (Catalyst Center) = CEO of the network. WLC is a regional manager (wireless only). DNA Center oversees wired, wireless, and WAN from one dashboard. Intent-based = you tell it the GOAL ('marketing gets priority to video servers') and it generates the ACLs, QoS, and VLAN configs automatically.
hack.practice: No Packet Tracer sim — DNA Center doesn't run in PT. Instead, watch Cisco's dCloud DNA Center demo (free) or the official sandbox. Focus on the four pillars: Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. Know the renaming to Catalyst Center (2023).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 60-61 (Network Automation) covers DNA Center. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29 (SDN). Exam question is nearly guaranteed — 'Cisco's IBN platform' = DNA/Catalyst Center.

1.1.e.info:
Cisco DNA Center / Catalyst Center organizes its functionality around four pillars. Memorizing them is the most efficient way to score DNA Center exam questions.

1. Design — Build the network model. Define site hierarchy (regions, buildings, floors). Create network profiles (device templates, IP address pools, SSIDs, wireless policies). Import floor plans for wireless heatmaps. Everything starts here — you can't provision what you haven't designed.

2. Policy — Express business intent as policies. Create groups (Users, Devices, Applications) and policies between them (who can talk to what, with what QoS). Under the hood, Cisco translates these into ACLs, QoS maps, and SGTs (Scalable Group Tags) for microsegmentation in SD-Access fabric. This is the 'intent' in intent-based networking.

3. Provision — Push configs to devices. Features:

  • Plug and Play (PnP) — zero-touch provisioning. New switches boot, call home to DNA Center, pull config/image automatically.
  • Template-based config — Jinja2 templates with variables pushed to many devices.
  • Software Image Management (SWIM) — manage and upgrade IOS/IOS-XE across fleet.
  • Fabric provisioning — build SD-Access fabric in minutes.

4. Assurance — Monitor and troubleshoot. Features:

  • Health scores — clients, devices, and applications get 0-10 health ratings.
  • AI/ML anomaly detection — flag deviations from baseline.
  • Root cause analysis — trace 'why is wireless slow in Building 3?' to specific interference or AP failure.
  • Path trace — visualize real traffic path between two endpoints.

Mnemonic: D-P-P-A: Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. 'Designing Policy, Provisioning Assurance.' Or 'DPPA — Deliver Premium Platform Automation.'

Exam hook: 'What are DNA Center's four pillars?' → Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. You'll also see matching questions: 'which pillar does PnP belong to?' → Provision.

children.name: Assurance — health, AI anomaly, root cause, path trace

hack.memory: Four pillars = D-P-P-A. Design (model the network) → Policy (define intent) → Provision (push it out) → Assurance (verify it's working). Memory hook: 'Designers Plan Products Always.' The pillars are sequential — you can't provision what you haven't designed, can't assure what you haven't provisioned.
hack.practice: Go to Cisco's public DNA Center demo (dcloud.cisco.com). Walk through each tab: Design, Policy, Provision, Assurance. Create a dummy site, define a wireless profile, push a template to a test switch, then check the Assurance dashboard. Even a 20-minute tour cements the four pillars.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 60-61 covers the pillars. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Expect a direct exam question: 'list DNA Center's four pillars' or 'which pillar is responsible for X?'

1.1.e.info:
SD-Access (Software-Defined Access) is Cisco's software-defined campus fabric architecture, orchestrated by DNA Center. It transforms a traditional hop-by-hop Layer 2/3 campus into a unified VXLAN overlay network with centralized policy and endpoint mobility.

Key components:

  • Underlay — the physical IP network: switches, routers, routing protocols (typically IS-IS). The underlay's only job is to provide IP reachability for the overlay. Usually /30 or /31 point-to-point links between switches running a routing protocol.
  • Overlay (VXLAN) — tunnels built on top of the underlay. Client traffic is encapsulated in VXLAN headers and tunneled between edge switches. Different VLANs at different sites can logically merge into a single subnet across the fabric.
  • LISP (Locator/ID Separation Protocol) — the control plane for endpoint tracking. When an endpoint moves from one fabric edge switch to another, LISP updates the mapping so traffic follows. Enables seamless mobility without broadcast-based MAC learning.
  • SGT (Scalable Group Tags) — tag endpoints with an identity (like 'Employee', 'Contractor', 'IoT_Camera'). Policy is enforced based on tags, not IPs — so policy persists even if the user moves subnets.
  • Cisco ISE — provides endpoint authentication and SGT assignment via 802.1X.

Why use SD-Access:

  • Campus-wide microsegmentation (group policies, not ACL sprawl).
  • Seamless roaming (endpoints keep IP when moving across the campus).
  • Consistent policy regardless of physical location.
  • Centralized visibility via DNA Center.

Trade-offs: Requires compatible Catalyst 9000 hardware. Higher complexity than traditional 3-tier campus. DNA Center license costs. For small sites, a traditional VLAN + L3 switch design is often simpler and cheaper.

Exam hook: 'What protocol does SD-Access use for data plane overlay?' → VXLAN. 'What protocol tracks endpoint location?' → LISP. 'What controller provisions SD-Access?' → DNA Center.

hack.memory: SD-Access = campus with GPS tracking. Underlay = the roads (physical IP links). Overlay (VXLAN) = GPS tunnels between any two points. LISP = the GPS database knowing where every car is. SGT = the car's sticker ('Employee', 'Guest'). Move anywhere on campus, policy follows the sticker, not the location.
hack.practice: No hands-on Packet Tracer — SDA doesn't simulate. Instead, draw a 3-tier campus with and without SD-Access. Annotate the overlay vs underlay. Learn the roles of DNA Center (brain), ISE (identity), LISP (control plane), VXLAN (data plane), SGT (policy tags). Flashcards for the acronyms work well.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 60-61 mentions SDA. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: low-to-medium weight. Know SDA = DNA Center fabric with VXLAN + LISP + SGT, and that Cisco ISE handles identity.

1.1.e.info:
This is the single most important WLC/DNA Center distinction on the CCNA — and a common exam trap. Know the scope difference cold.

WLC (Wireless LAN Controller):

  • Manages wireless only: APs, WLANs, wireless clients.
  • Pushes wireless configuration to lightweight APs via CAPWAP.
  • Performs wireless-specific functions: RRM, rogue detection, client authentication proxy, CAPWAP tunneling.
  • Does NOT manage switches, routers, or wired policy.
  • Examples: Catalyst 9800, AireOS 5520, 3504, Mobility Express.

DNA Center / Catalyst Center:

  • Manages the entire enterprise network: wired switches, routers, WLCs (and through them, APs), SD-WAN.
  • Intent-based networking orchestrator.
  • Provides automation, assurance, policy across all devices.
  • Runs on dedicated appliances, typically 3-node cluster for HA.
  • Can provision an SD-Access fabric end-to-end.

Hierarchy in practice: DNA Center manages the WLC as one of many devices. The WLC manages APs. APs serve wireless clients. DNA Center does NOT directly manage APs — it goes through the WLC.

Exam traps to watch for:

  • 'Which controller manages lightweight APs?' → WLC (not DNA Center).
  • 'Which platform provides intent-based networking across the enterprise?' → DNA Center.
  • 'Which manages both wired and wireless?' → DNA Center.
  • 'Which provides CAPWAP termination?' → WLC.

Version naming: DNA Center was renamed Catalyst Center in 2023. Both names refer to the same product. The CCNA may use either term.

hack.memory: WLC = wireless-only sheriff. DNA Center = state governor managing sheriffs (WLCs), police chiefs (switch admins), and highway patrol (routers). Scope is the key difference — WLC scope is narrow and deep (all things wireless); DNA Center scope is wide and strategic (all things network).
hack.practice: Make a 2-column comparison table. Row 1: 'Manages APs?' → WLC: yes directly, DNA Center: indirectly via WLC. Row 2: 'Manages switches?' → WLC: no, DNA Center: yes. Row 3: 'Intent-based?' → WLC: no, DNA Center: yes. Row 4: 'CAPWAP?' → WLC: yes, DNA Center: no. Drill these until automatic.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57 (WLC) and Day 60-61 (DNA Center). Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 26 and 29. This distinction appears on nearly every CCNA attempt — make it automatic.

1.1.f.info:
An endpoint is any device at the edge of a network that originates or consumes data — the source and destination of communication. Endpoints are the reason the network exists; everything else (switches, routers, firewalls) is infrastructure that moves data between endpoints.

The classification test: Does the device generate or consume data? Yes → endpoint. Does it just forward data? → infrastructure. A PC generates web requests → endpoint. A printer receives print jobs → endpoint. A switch moves frames without caring about their content → infrastructure. This test decides every 'is X an endpoint?' exam question.

Common endpoint categories:

  • User devices: PCs, laptops, tablets, smartphones.
  • Communication devices: VoIP phones, video conferencing endpoints.
  • Peripherals: network printers, scanners.
  • IoT devices: IP cameras, smart thermostats, badge readers, environmental sensors, industrial controllers (PLCs), medical devices, smart TVs.
  • Servers: technically endpoints too, though they're often treated as a separate category because they provide services.

Endpoint characteristics: Every endpoint has a NIC with a MAC address, an IP address (typically DHCP-assigned), and a default gateway. Most endpoints send and receive traffic directed to their own unicast address; exceptions include multicast receivers (IPTV, streaming) and broadcast generators (DHCP clients, ARP).

Endpoints as the attack surface: Endpoints are the #1 target for malware, phishing, and credential theft because they're where humans interact with the network. An attacker who compromises an endpoint can then move laterally into the network. This is why endpoint security (EDR, NAC, patching, 802.1X auth) gets so much attention in modern security programs.

Exam hook: 'Which of the following is an endpoint?' with a list including PC, switch, router, IP camera — answer: PC and IP camera (the data generators/consumers). Switches and routers are infrastructure.

hack.memory: Endpoint = anyone who SPEAKS or LISTENS on the network. Infrastructure = the hallways and doors. PCs talk, printers listen, cameras stream — endpoints. Switches and routers just move the conversation along — infrastructure. If it has a user-facing purpose, it's probably an endpoint.
hack.practice: Make a 2-column flashcard. Column A: 15 devices (PC, laptop, IP phone, printer, IP camera, router, switch, firewall, WLC, AP, server, IoT sensor, smart TV, tablet, thermostat). Column B: Endpoint or Infrastructure. Classify each. Drill until instant.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 1 (Networking Basics) introduces endpoints. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 1. Low-weight exam topic — expect a single 'identify the endpoint' question. The concept underpins all security/access-layer topics that follow.

1.1.f.info:
A NIC (Network Interface Card) is the hardware that connects a device to the network — wired (Ethernet) or wireless (Wi-Fi). Every NIC has a unique MAC address (Media Access Control) — a 48-bit / 6-byte identifier burned into the NIC's ROM at manufacturing. This is also called the BIA (Burned-In Address).

MAC format: 48 bits, written as 6 pairs of hex digits separated by colons or dashes: 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E or 001A.2B3C.4D5E (Cisco dotted notation). The first 24 bits = OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier) assigned to the manufacturer (e.g., Cisco has several OUIs including 00:0B:BE). The last 24 bits = vendor-assigned serial. Together they theoretically guarantee uniqueness worldwide, though in practice vendors sometimes duplicate MACs.

Special MAC addresses:

  • Broadcast: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF — all hosts in the broadcast domain.
  • Multicast (IPv4): 01:00:5E:xx:xx:xx — mapped from IPv4 multicast group.
  • Multicast (IPv6): 33:33:xx:xx:xx:xx.
  • Locally administered: 2nd bit of 1st byte = 1. Indicates admin-set (not burned-in) — used by virtual NICs, containers.

MAC vs IP: MAC operates at Layer 2, bounded by the local broadcast domain (doesn't traverse routers). IP operates at Layer 3, globally unique (within its scope). A packet keeps its IP unchanged end-to-end, but its MAC is rewritten at every router hop.

Changing MAC (spoofing): Modern OSs can override the burned-in MAC in software — ifconfig eth0 hw ether 00:11:22:33:44:55 on Linux. This is called MAC spoofing. Used for legitimate purposes (virtualization, privacy on public Wi-Fi) and malicious purposes (bypassing MAC filtering, impersonating authorized devices).

Exam hook: 'How many bits in a MAC address?' → 48. 'What does OUI identify?' → the manufacturer. 'What's the broadcast MAC?' → FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF.

hack.memory: MAC = NIC's license plate. 48 bits split in half: first 24 = manufacturer (OUI, like the car make), last 24 = vendor serial (like the car's VIN). Broadcast MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF (shouting to everyone). Software can spoof the plate, but physically burned-in at manufacture.
hack.practice: On your own computer: run 'ipconfig /all' (Windows) or 'ifconfig' (Mac/Linux) and find your NIC's MAC. Look up the OUI at wireshark.org/tools/oui-lookup.html — you'll see your hardware manufacturer. In Packet Tracer, check any device's interface and note the MAC format. Memorize that first 3 bytes = OUI.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 6 (Ethernet). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 5. Exam: guaranteed question on MAC format (48 bits) and structure (OUI + vendor). Also broadcast MAC. Know the hex notation conventions (colons or dots).

1.1.f.info:
The default gateway is the IP address of the router (or L3 switch SVI) that an endpoint sends traffic to when the destination is outside its own local subnet. Without a default gateway, endpoints can only talk to other hosts on the same subnet — they'd be cut off from the rest of the network and the internet.

How an endpoint decides when to use it: When sending a packet, the host compares the destination IP to its own subnet (using its IP + subnet mask). If the destination is inside the local subnet, the host ARPs for the destination MAC and sends directly. If outside, the host ARPs for the DEFAULT GATEWAY's MAC and sends the frame to the gateway — even though the IP header still shows the real destination. The gateway then routes the packet toward its destination.

Configuration — static:

Windows: ipconfig shows 'Default Gateway'
Linux:   route add default gw 10.0.10.1
Cisco:   ip default-gateway 10.0.10.1  (for L2 switch management)

Configuration — via DHCP: The DHCP server hands out the default gateway (DHCP option 3) along with IP, mask, and DNS. This is how 99% of endpoints learn their gateway — automatically.

Common problems:

  • Missing default gateway → endpoint can ping its own subnet but nothing else.
  • Wrong default gateway → packets go to the wrong router; return traffic may fail.
  • Gateway unreachable (router down) → clients cannot reach anything off-subnet. Mitigated by HSRP/VRRP first-hop redundancy.

Default gateway vs default route: On an endpoint, it's called the 'default gateway.' On a router, the same concept is the 'default route' (0.0.0.0/0). Different term, same function: where do I send traffic when I don't have a specific route.

Exam hook: 'What IP does an endpoint send traffic to when the destination is outside its subnet?' → default gateway. 'If a PC can ping other PCs on its subnet but not the internet, what's the likely issue?' → missing or wrong default gateway.

hack.memory: Default gateway = your nearest exit from the neighborhood. Packet for your street? Deliver direct. Packet for another city? Hand it to the gateway router at the neighborhood's edge. Missing gateway = stuck inside your subnet forever. Wrong gateway = mail goes to the wrong post office.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure a PC with IP 10.0.10.5/24 and no gateway. Try to ping 8.8.8.8 — fails. Add gateway 10.0.10.1 (the router's interface). Ping again — works (assuming route to the internet exists). Now change gateway to 10.0.10.99 (nonexistent) — pings fail again. This 3-step drill burns in 'gateway = exit router.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 8 (Routing) introduces default gateway. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Guaranteed exam question — troubleshooting scenarios almost always include a default gateway check.

1.1.f.info:
The access layer is the bottom tier of the Cisco three-tier campus model — the layer where endpoints physically connect to the network. Its job is port-level connectivity and enforcement: every PC, phone, printer, and AP plugs into an access-layer switch port.

Typical access switches: Cisco Catalyst 9200, 9300-L, 2960 (legacy), Meraki MS. These are L2-focused — they usually don't run routing protocols or perform inter-VLAN routing (that's the distribution layer's job).

Access layer responsibilities:

  • VLAN assignment — every port is assigned to a VLAN (user, voice, IoT, guest, etc.), or uses 802.1X dynamic VLAN assignment.
  • Port security — limit the number of MACs per port; shut/restrict violators.
  • 802.1X authentication — require credentials before allowing network access.
  • DHCP snooping — trust only specific ports to send DHCP offers.
  • Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) — prevent ARP spoofing.
  • PoE delivery — power IP phones, APs, cameras over Ethernet.
  • QoS trust boundary — mark/re-mark traffic at the first switch (trust the phone's CoS/DSCP, ignore PC DSCP, etc.).
  • STP edge / BPDU Guard — treat access ports as leaf nodes (PortFast) and shut down any port that receives a BPDU.

Scale: Access layer has the most switches in any campus — a building with 1000 users might have 30+ access switches, each with 24-48 ports. Keep the config simple and uniform; automate with templates.

Uplinks: Each access switch has 1-4 uplinks to distribution switches (typically 10G or faster, often aggregated via EtherChannel). Dual-homing (uplinking to BOTH distribution switches) provides redundancy.

Exam hook: 'Where do endpoints connect in a three-tier campus?' → access layer. 'Which layer enforces port security and 802.1X?' → access layer.

children.name: QoS trust boundary + STP edge (PortFast/BPDU Guard)

hack.memory: Access = where the wires meet the users. Every endpoint's cable plugs into an access-layer switch. Access = enforcement layer: port security, 802.1X, DHCP snooping, DAI, PortFast. Keep the config simple and uniform — you'll have a LOT of access switches.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build a small access-layer setup. 2960 switch, 5 PCs. Configure VLAN 10 + port security (max 1 MAC, shutdown on violation), 'spanning-tree portfast' on access ports, 'spanning-tree bpduguard enable'. Plug a random device into an access port and verify the port shuts if violated. This is a real access-layer template.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24 (LAN Architecture) covers layers. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: near-guaranteed 'match layer to function' question — access = endpoints + port policy.

1.1.f.info:
Port security is a Layer 2 access-layer feature that limits which MAC addresses can connect on a given switch port and/or how many MACs can be learned. It's the first line of defense against random devices plugging into exposed network jacks.

Configuration (Cisco):

interface Gi0/1
 switchport mode access
 switchport port-security                     ! enable
 switchport port-security maximum 1             ! only 1 MAC per port
 switchport port-security violation shutdown    ! protect | restrict | shutdown
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky    ! learn then save to running-config

Three violation modes:

  • Protect — silently drop frames from unauthorized MACs. No alert, no port shutdown.
  • Restrict — drop frames AND log via syslog + increment violation counter.
  • Shutdown (default) — err-disable the port. Port goes 'err-disabled' and requires manual intervention (shutdown; no shutdown) or err-disable auto-recovery.

MAC learning types:

  • Static — explicitly configured: switchport port-security mac-address aaaa.bbbb.cccc. Survives reboot.
  • Dynamic — learned from traffic. Lost on reboot.
  • Sticky — learned from traffic but added to running-config. Saves with write memory. Best of both worlds.

Maximum MACs: Default is 1. For a port serving a PC behind an IP phone (voice + data VLANs), set max to 2 or 3.

Verification: show port-security, show port-security interface Gi0/1, show port-security address (all learned + static MACs).

Recovery from err-disable: Manual shutdown / no shutdown, or auto-recovery: errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation and errdisable recovery interval 300.

hack.memory: Port security = bouncer at a specific door. 'Only certain people allowed. Too many? Take action.' Max MACs + action (protect/restrict/shutdown). Sticky MAC = bouncer writes down regulars so he remembers them tomorrow. Default shutdown mode = err-disabled port until admin unlocks it.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure port security on Gi0/1 with max 1 and violation shutdown. Connect PC1 — works. Replace with PC2 — port goes err-disabled. Run 'show interfaces status' and see 'err-disabled'. Recover with shutdown/no shutdown. Now set sticky MAC and 'write memory' — reload and verify the MAC persists.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 49 (Port Security). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 7. High exam weight — expect config and troubleshooting questions. Know the 3 violation modes, sticky MAC, default max of 1.

1.1.f.info:
IEEE 802.1X is the industry-standard port-based network access control protocol. Before any traffic is allowed on a switch port (or wireless SSID), the connecting endpoint must authenticate — typically with a username/password or a certificate. Without valid credentials, the port stays in an 'unauthorized' state that blocks all traffic except EAPOL (the authentication protocol itself).

Three roles:

  • Supplicant — the endpoint requesting access (laptop, phone). Runs 802.1X client software.
  • Authenticator — the switch or WLC. Relays authentication messages between supplicant and authentication server.
  • Authentication Server — RADIUS server (typically Cisco ISE, FreeRADIUS, or Microsoft NPS) that validates credentials.

Protocol stack:

  • Between supplicant and switch: EAPOL (EAP over LAN).
  • Between switch and RADIUS: RADIUS protocol carrying EAP.
  • Multiple EAP methods: EAP-TLS (certificate), PEAP (tunneled password), EAP-FAST, EAP-TTLS.

Configuration (Cisco switch basics):

aaa new-model
aaa authentication dot1x default group radius
aaa authorization network default group radius
dot1x system-auth-control
radius server ISE
 address ipv4 10.0.1.10 auth-port 1812 acct-port 1813
 key MyRadiusSecret
interface Gi0/1
 switchport mode access
 authentication port-control auto
 dot1x pae authenticator

Authentication results:

  • Success → switch applies VLAN assignment, ACLs, SGTs per the RADIUS response.
  • Failure → port stays unauthorized. Optional: 'guest VLAN' for failed/missing supplicants.
  • Timeout → 'auth-fail VLAN' or 'restricted VLAN' assignment.

MAB (MAC Authentication Bypass) — for devices that don't support 802.1X (printers, older IoT), the switch can fall back to authenticating via MAC address alone. Configure with mab under the interface.

Exam hook: 'What port-based authentication standard?' → 802.1X. 'What are the 3 roles?' → supplicant, authenticator, authentication server.

hack.memory: 802.1X = ID check at the door before any access. 3 roles: Supplicant (the guest), Authenticator (the bouncer/switch), Auth Server (the guest list keeper/RADIUS). Until accepted, only EAPOL squeaks through — no general traffic. Fallback MAB for printers and IoT that can't do 802.1X.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure 802.1X on a switch port pointing to a RADIUS server. Set up a user on the server and a supplicant on the PC. Connect and watch the EAPOL exchange in Simulation mode. Try with wrong creds → port stays unauthorized. Try with right creds → access granted.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54 (Security) covers 802.1X. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Expect an exam question on the 3 roles and the authentication server being RADIUS. MAB is a bonus concept.

1.1.f.info:
DHCP snooping is a Layer 2 security feature that prevents rogue DHCP servers from handing out IPs to clients on the network. A rogue DHCP server (malicious or misconfigured) could give clients a bad gateway (pointing to an attacker's machine), wrong DNS (pointing to phishing sites), or simply break connectivity. DHCP snooping blocks this by restricting which ports may send DHCP server messages.

Core concepts:

  • Trusted port — allowed to send DHCP server messages (OFFER, ACK, NAK). Configured explicitly where your real DHCP server is connected, or on uplinks leading there.
  • Untrusted port (default) — cannot send DHCP server messages. Any OFFER or ACK from an untrusted port is dropped. Clients on these ports can send DISCOVER/REQUEST (client messages) normally.

Configuration:

ip dhcp snooping                           ! global enable
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20,30             ! enable for specific VLANs
interface Gi0/24                            ! uplink to DHCP server
 ip dhcp snooping trust

All other ports are untrusted by default.

Snooping binding table: DHCP snooping builds a table mapping client MAC → IP → VLAN → port → lease time. This table is valuable beyond just rogue prevention — it's the data source for Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) and IP Source Guard. Verify with show ip dhcp snooping binding.

Option 82 (DHCP Relay Info): DHCP snooping can insert Option 82 into DHCP requests (containing VLAN and port info). The DHCP server can use this to assign policy per port. Some DHCP servers reject requests with Option 82 from relay — use ip dhcp snooping information option allow-untrusted to handle.

Rate limiting: ip dhcp snooping limit rate [N] — prevents DHCP starvation attacks (flooding DISCOVER to exhaust a pool).

Exam hook: 'What feature prevents rogue DHCP servers?' → DHCP snooping. 'Which port configuration allows a real DHCP server to respond?' → trust.

hack.memory: DHCP snooping = bouncer checking 'who can hand out tables here?' Trusted port (default gateway's VLAN uplink) = the real DHCP server. Every other port is untrusted — any DHCP OFFER from there gets dropped. Side benefit: builds a binding table that other security features use.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: enable DHCP snooping globally and on VLAN 10. Mark the uplink to the real DHCP server as trusted. Set up a rogue DHCP server on another port and try to lease to a client — the client gets blocked/only reaches the real server. Run 'show ip dhcp snooping binding' to see the table.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54 covers DHCP snooping. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. High exam weight — know 'trust' direction points at the real DHCP server, default is untrusted.

1.1.f.info:
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) is a Layer 2 security feature that prevents ARP spoofing / poisoning attacks. Without DAI, an attacker can send fake ARP replies claiming to be the default gateway — intercepting all off-subnet traffic (man-in-the-middle). DAI validates every ARP packet against a trusted binding table (the DHCP snooping binding table) and drops any that don't match.

How ARP spoofing works (the attack): Attacker sends gratuitous ARPs: 'I am 10.0.10.1 (gateway), MAC is aa:aa:aa'. Victim updates its ARP cache. Now the victim's off-subnet traffic goes to the attacker's MAC, the attacker reads/modifies it, forwards to real gateway. Classic MITM.

How DAI blocks it: DAI intercepts ARP packets on untrusted ports and checks: does this MAC + IP binding match the DHCP snooping table? If yes → forward. If no → drop the ARP and log.

Configuration:

ip dhcp snooping                               ! DAI depends on this
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10
ip arp inspection vlan 10                       ! enable DAI for VLAN 10
interface Gi0/24
 ip arp inspection trust                        ! trust uplinks (like DHCP snooping)
interface range Gi0/1 - 23
 ip arp inspection limit rate 15                ! rate limit on untrusted

Uplink and switch-to-switch ports should be trusted. Endpoint-facing ports are untrusted (default).

Non-DHCP hosts (static IPs): For hosts that don't get DHCP (servers with static IPs), create an ARP ACL binding MAC to IP statically: arp access-list SERVERS + permit ip host 10.0.10.100 mac host aaaa.bbbb.cccc. Then apply: ip arp inspection filter SERVERS vlan 10.

Dependencies: DAI requires DHCP snooping to be enabled — it needs the snooping binding table as its source of truth. DAI alone without DHCP snooping will drop everything.

Exam hook: 'What prevents ARP spoofing / MITM?' → DAI. 'What does DAI depend on?' → DHCP snooping binding table.

hack.memory: DAI = lie detector for ARP packets. Uses the DHCP snooping binding table as ground truth. Attacker sends fake ARP ('I am the gateway'), DAI checks the table, sees no match, drops. Must pair with DHCP snooping — DAI without snooping has nothing to verify against.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: enable DHCP snooping + DAI on VLAN 10. Let a client DHCP normally (binding table populates). Now use a 'Laptop' with a packet crafter to send a spoofed ARP ('I am gateway with MAC aaaa...'). DAI drops it. Run 'show ip arp inspection statistics' to see the drop counter.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. High exam weight. Know DAI and DHCP snooping as a PAIR — DAI depends on DHCP snooping binding table.

1.1.f.info:
Network Access Control (NAC) is a broader security concept where devices must be verified — for identity AND compliance — before being granted network access. 802.1X provides the identity piece; NAC extends it to also check device health: OS version, antivirus status, patch level, disk encryption, domain membership, etc.

Typical NAC workflow:

  1. Device connects to switch/wireless.
  2. 802.1X authenticates the user/device identity (supplicant → authenticator → RADIUS).
  3. NAC posture check — a NAC agent on the device reports compliance data back to the NAC server (Cisco ISE, Aruba ClearPass, FortiNAC).
  4. Based on identity + posture, the NAC server assigns access: full, restricted (quarantine VLAN for remediation), or denied.
  5. Remediation — devices in quarantine can reach patch/AV update servers only. Once fixed, recheck and promote to full access.

Two NAC modes:

  • Agent-based — NAC client software on the endpoint reports posture. More accurate but requires deployment.
  • Agentless — NAC server probes the endpoint remotely (OS fingerprinting, scans). Less intrusive but less thorough.

Why NAC matters: 802.1X alone answers 'who are you?' NAC also answers 'is your device safe enough to let on the network?' Prevents compromised but authenticated laptops from spreading malware internally.

Cisco's NAC platform: Cisco Identity Services Engine (ISE) — next entry.

Exam hook: 'What provides device posture + identity checking before granting access?' → NAC. 'What's the difference between 802.1X and NAC?' → 802.1X = identity only; NAC = identity + posture.

hack.memory: NAC = TSA pre-check. 802.1X shows your ID (identity). NAC also checks your shoes, laptop, and liquids (posture). Pass both → boarding pass. Fail posture → remediation room until you fix it. Without NAC, a validly-authenticated but infected laptop can still wreck your LAN.
hack.practice: Watch a Cisco ISE demo on dCloud — walk through 802.1X + posture. Make a flashcard: 802.1X = identity (who), NAC = identity + posture (who + safe?). Remediation VLAN is a common NAC pattern — label it too.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Medium exam weight. Know NAC concept and that Cisco ISE is Cisco's NAC platform.

1.1.f.info:
Cisco Identity Services Engine (ISE) is Cisco's centralized AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting), 802.1X, profiling, and posture platform. It's the enforcement engine behind Cisco NAC and the identity source for SD-Access. Nearly every modern Cisco enterprise deployment pairs ISE with the WLC, switches, and DNA Center.

Core capabilities:

  • AAA / RADIUS server — validates 802.1X, VPN, and device admin credentials.
  • Device profiling — fingerprints connecting devices (printers, phones, cameras) using DHCP, HTTP, CDP, LLDP, SNMP, RADIUS attributes. Auto-categorizes into device profile classes.
  • Posture assessment — checks OS version, AV status, patches, encryption via the AnyConnect or ISE Posture agent.
  • Guest and BYOD portals — self-service captive portal for visitors and personal devices.
  • Scalable Group Tags (SGT) — assigns identity-based tags used in SD-Access microsegmentation.
  • TACACS+ — for device admin access (often paired with 802.1X RADIUS for endpoint access).

Deployment: Virtual or physical appliance. HA cluster of ISE nodes for redundancy. Integrates with AD/LDAP for user directory, Active Directory Certificate Services for cert-based EAP-TLS, and MDM platforms for BYOD.

Integration points:

  • WLC → ISE (wireless auth).
  • Switches → ISE (wired 802.1X / MAB).
  • Firewalls → ISE (identity-based firewall policy via pxGrid).
  • DNA Center → ISE (SD-Access policy translation).
  • VPN → ISE (remote access auth).

Exam hook: 'Which Cisco product provides centralized 802.1X + NAC policy?' → ISE. 'What connects identity to SGTs in SD-Access?' → ISE. Low-to-medium weight, but near-guaranteed in any security/architecture question.

hack.memory: ISE = Cisco's identity brain. Answers 'who are you, what device, is it safe, what group?' Feeds all of that to switches, WLCs, firewalls, and DNA Center. Pairs with 802.1X (gatekeeper) and NAC (health check). If you see 'RADIUS + NAC + Cisco' in a question, the answer is ISE.
hack.practice: Tour an ISE demo on dCloud. Walk through creating an Authorization Profile, a policy set, and a device profile. Identify how ISE talks to a switch (RADIUS 1812/1813), to endpoints (profiling), and to DNA Center (pxGrid). Make a cheat sheet mapping each ISE capability to the protocol or integration used.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54 mentions ISE briefly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Exam: recognize ISE as Cisco's AAA/NAC platform. Name-recognition is sufficient for CCNA.

1.1.g.info:
The client-server model is the dominant architecture pattern for network services. A client initiates a request for a service; a server listens on a well-known port and responds. The client knows how to find the server; the server waits passively for clients to contact it.

How the model works:

  1. The server process binds to a specific well-known port (HTTP = 80, SSH = 22, DNS = 53, etc.) and listens.
  2. The client opens a socket, assigns itself a random high-numbered ephemeral port (typically 49152-65535), and sends a request to the server's IP + well-known port.
  3. The server processes the request and sends a response to the client's IP + ephemeral port.
  4. The conversation is uniquely identified by the 5-tuple: (client IP, client port, server IP, server port, protocol).

Well-known ports (0-1023): reserved for standard services like HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), SSH (22). Managed by IANA.
Registered ports (1024-49151): assigned to specific applications (3306 MySQL, 5432 Postgres, 3389 RDP).
Ephemeral ports (49152-65535): dynamically assigned to clients for each connection.

Client-server vs peer-to-peer: In peer-to-peer (P2P) networks, every node is both client and server — file sharing (BitTorrent), blockchain, some VoIP. The CCNA exam focuses on client-server; P2P is acknowledged but not deeply tested.

Stateful vs stateless: HTTP is stateless — each request is independent. FTP is stateful — the connection maintains session state. Databases, SMTP, SSH are all stateful. Stateless services scale horizontally more easily.

Exam hook: 'In client-server, which side listens on a well-known port?' → server. 'What port range do clients typically use for source ports?' → ephemeral (49152-65535).

hack.memory: Client = customer walking into a store. Server = storekeeper waiting behind a known counter (port 80, 443, 53, 22). Customer uses a random seat number (ephemeral port 49152+). The 5-tuple (client IP+port, server IP+port, protocol) uniquely identifies every conversation. Server is passive (waiting); client initiates.
hack.practice: On your laptop, run 'netstat -an' (or 'ss -tuln' on Linux). Notice LISTEN sockets on well-known ports (80 if you're running a webserver locally, 22 for SSH). Open a browser to any site and immediately run netstat again — you'll see an ESTABLISHED connection from your machine's ephemeral port to the remote server's port 443. That's the 5-tuple in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 3 (TCP/IP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Exam: understand the model and port ranges. Pair with the port-number memorization from the next entries.

1.1.g.info:
DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable names (google.com) into IP addresses (142.250.190.14). Without DNS, every connection would require you to type raw IPs. DNS uses UDP port 53 for most queries and TCP port 53 for large responses (zone transfers, DNSSEC responses that exceed 512 bytes).

DNS resolution flow:

  1. Client asks its configured resolver (e.g., 8.8.8.8): 'What is google.com?'
  2. Resolver checks its cache. Cached? Return immediately.
  3. Not cached → ask a root server (.) → root points to the .com TLD server.
  4. .com TLD server points to google.com's authoritative name servers.
  5. Authoritative server answers: '142.250.190.14'.
  6. Resolver caches the answer (respecting TTL) and returns to client.

Record types (memorize for CCNA):

  • A — IPv4 address (hostname → 32-bit IP).
  • AAAA — IPv6 address (hostname → 128-bit IP).
  • CNAME — Canonical name / alias (one name points to another name).
  • MX — Mail exchange (what server handles email for this domain).
  • NS — Name server (which server is authoritative for this zone).
  • PTR — Pointer / reverse DNS (IP → hostname).
  • TXT — arbitrary text (SPF, DKIM, domain verification).
  • SOA — Start of Authority (zone metadata).

On Cisco IOS: Configure DNS with ip name-server 8.8.8.8 and enable with ip domain-lookup. If you mistype a command, IOS treats the typo as a hostname and tries to resolve — add no ip domain-lookup to kill that annoying hang on a lab device.

Exam hook: 'What port does DNS use?' → UDP 53 (TCP 53 for large). 'Which DNS record type maps hostname to IPv4?' → A. 'To IPv6?' → AAAA. 'For mail?' → MX.

hack.memory: DNS = phonebook of the internet. UDP 53 for quick lookups, TCP 53 for big transfers. Record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6 = 4×A = 4 times bigger), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), PTR (reverse). On Cisco: 'no ip domain-lookup' kills the annoying typo-DNS hang.
hack.practice: Run 'nslookup google.com' (any OS) — note the A record. 'nslookup -type=AAAA google.com' — IPv6. 'nslookup -type=MX gmail.com' — mail servers. 'nslookup -type=PTR 8.8.8.8' — reverse. In Packet Tracer: configure a DNS server with A records for lab hostnames, set client DNS server, and ping by hostname.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 39 (DNS). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 13. Very high exam weight — know the port (UDP/TCP 53) and all 5-6 record types.

1.1.g.info:
DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automates IP address assignment and configuration delivery to endpoints. Without DHCP, every device would need manual IP/mask/gateway/DNS configuration — untenable at scale. DHCP uses UDP port 67 (server) and UDP port 68 (client).

DORA — the 4-step handshake:

  1. D — Discover — Client broadcasts 'DHCPDISCOVER' (src 0.0.0.0:68, dst 255.255.255.255:67). 'Anyone out there? I need an IP.'
  2. O — Offer — Server replies (unicast or broadcast back): 'DHCPOFFER' with a proposed IP, mask, gateway, DNS, lease time.
  3. R — Request — Client broadcasts 'DHCPREQUEST' formally accepting one of the offers (important if multiple servers responded).
  4. A — Acknowledge — Server sends 'DHCPACK' finalizing the lease. Client can now use the IP.

Options delivered beyond IP/mask:

  • Option 3 — Default gateway (router).
  • Option 6 — DNS server(s).
  • Option 51 — Lease duration (e.g., 24 hours).
  • Option 43 — Vendor-specific (used for AP→WLC discovery).
  • Option 66/67 — TFTP server + bootfile (PXE boot, IP phones).
  • Option 150 — TFTP server IP (Cisco IP phones).

DHCP relay (ip helper-address): Routers block broadcasts, so a client in one subnet can't reach a DHCP server in another. Configure ip helper-address [server-ip] on the router's client-facing interface — the router converts the broadcast to a unicast and forwards to the server.

Cisco IOS DHCP server config:

ip dhcp excluded-address 10.0.10.1 10.0.10.10
ip dhcp pool VLAN10
 network 10.0.10.0 255.255.255.0
 default-router 10.0.10.1
 dns-server 8.8.8.8
 lease 7

Exam hook: 'What does DORA stand for?' → Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge. 'What ports does DHCP use?' → UDP 67 server, 68 client. 'How do you relay DHCP across a router?' → 'ip helper-address' on the client-facing interface.

hack.memory: DHCP = auto-valet for IPs. DORA dance: Discover (shout 'need table!') → Offer (server: 'take table 5') → Request (client: 'I'll take it') → Acknowledge (server: 'confirmed'). Ports 67 server, 68 client. Need to cross a router? 'ip helper-address' turns broadcast into unicast relay.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build a router + 2 subnets. DHCP server on subnet A. Client on subnet B. Initially, client can't get an IP because the DHCPDISCOVER doesn't cross the router. Add 'ip helper-address [server-ip]' on the router's B-side interface — client gets an IP. Capture the DORA exchange in Simulation mode.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 38. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 13. Near-guaranteed exam question on DORA and ports 67/68. Also expect 'ip helper-address' troubleshooting scenarios.

1.1.g.info:
HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the protocol behind the World Wide Web. A client sends a request (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.) to a web server, which returns a response containing headers and (usually) a body. HTTP uses TCP port 80 and sends everything in plaintext.

HTTPS is HTTP wrapped in TLS (Transport Layer Security), the modern successor to SSL. Uses TCP port 443. Provides three guarantees: (1) confidentiality (encrypted payload), (2) integrity (tampering detection), (3) authentication (server proves its identity with a digital certificate signed by a trusted CA).

TLS handshake (simplified):

  1. Client sends ClientHello with supported cipher suites and TLS versions.
  2. Server responds with ServerHello, chosen cipher, and its certificate (x.509 containing its public key).
  3. Client validates certificate against trusted CA list.
  4. Key exchange (RSA historically, ECDHE today) generates a shared symmetric session key.
  5. Both sides switch to symmetric encryption. Application data (HTTP) flows encrypted.

HTTP methods to know:

  • GET — retrieve data. Idempotent.
  • POST — submit data, create resource.
  • PUT — update/replace resource.
  • DELETE — remove resource.
  • HEAD — like GET but headers only.
  • OPTIONS — what methods does this endpoint support?

HTTP status codes: 2xx success (200 OK), 3xx redirect (301, 302), 4xx client error (404 Not Found, 403 Forbidden, 401 Unauthorized), 5xx server error (500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable).

HTTPS caveats: HTTPS encrypts the payload but NOT the TLS SNI (server name) on older TLS versions, NOT the DNS query that resolved the hostname, NOT the destination IP/port. Network monitors can still see WHERE you went, just not WHAT you sent.

Exam hook: 'HTTP port?' → TCP 80. 'HTTPS port?' → TCP 443. 'What provides encryption for HTTPS?' → TLS (formerly SSL).

hack.memory: HTTP = postcard mail (anyone can read it). HTTPS = sealed envelope with tamper-proof wax + notarized identity (TLS + cert). Port 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS. Memory: 'Eighty is naked, four-four-three is fully dressed.'
hack.practice: Open Wireshark on any website visit. Filter for 'http' — you'll see HTTP requests in cleartext on port 80. Filter for 'tls' — you'll see the TLS handshake (ClientHello, ServerHello, certificate) before encrypted data. Notice the SNI field in ClientHello — even HTTPS reveals the hostname you're connecting to.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 4. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 4. Near-guaranteed port-memorization question. Know ports, the HTTPS = HTTP + TLS distinction, and major status code families.

1.1.g.info:
Two file transfer protocols with very different use cases and risk profiles.

FTP (File Transfer Protocol): The classic file transfer protocol. Uses TWO ports:

  • TCP port 21 — Control channel: commands (USER, PASS, RETR, STOR, LIST), session management.
  • TCP port 20 — Data channel (active mode): actual file bytes. In active mode, the server initiates the data connection back to the client.
  • Passive mode: client initiates both connections (to port 21 for control, to a random server port announced via PASV command for data). Needed when client is behind NAT/firewall.

FTP sends usernames, passwords, and data all in plaintext. Any network sniffer can capture credentials. FTP is largely obsolete for new deployments.

SFTP (SSH File Transfer Protocol): Not related to FTP — it's file transfer OVER SSH (TCP port 22). Fully encrypted. The modern replacement for FTP.

FTPS (FTP over TLS): Adds TLS to FTP. Less common than SFTP.

TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol): A bare-bones transfer protocol using UDP port 69. Key characteristics:

  • No authentication — anyone who can reach the server can read/write.
  • No encryption — plaintext data.
  • Simple lock-step protocol (request → data block → ACK → next block).
  • Connectionless (UDP) but reliable via ACKs.

TFTP's network role: Despite its insecurity, TFTP is ubiquitous in networking for transferring Cisco IOS images and configuration files to/from devices. Also used for PXE boot (DHCP + TFTP → client downloads boot image) and IP phone provisioning (phones pull config from TFTP server specified by DHCP Option 150).

Cisco IOS TFTP commands: copy running-config tftp://10.0.0.5/router-backup.cfg, copy tftp://10.0.0.5/new-ios.bin flash:, ip tftp source-interface Gi0/0.

Exam hook: 'FTP ports?' → TCP 21 control, TCP 20 data. 'TFTP port?' → UDP 69. 'Secure FTP alternative?' → SFTP (over SSH port 22). 'What transfers IOS images?' → TFTP (traditionally).

right.label: TFTP (UDP 69)
items: Used for IOS images, PXE, IP phones

hack.memory: FTP = two channels (21 control + 20 data), plaintext, evil. TFTP = one channel on UDP 69, no auth, used for network plumbing (IOS images, PXE boot). SFTP = file transfer on SSH (port 22), secure. If you need security: SFTP. If you're booting a switch: TFTP.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: place a TFTP server, upload a file. From a router, 'copy running-config tftp:' and specify the server's IP. Verify the file lands on the server. This is the most common real-world TFTP operation. For FTP, install FileZilla client + server locally and capture the plaintext login in Wireshark — USER and PASS visible.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 42 (FTP/TFTP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 13. Expect port memorization questions. Know FTP insecurity and TFTP's role in IOS transfer + IP phone provisioning.

1.1.g.info:
Both SSH and Telnet provide remote CLI access to network devices. The critical difference: SSH is encrypted, Telnet is plaintext. In modern networks, you should NEVER use Telnet over an untrusted network.

Telnet (TCP port 23):

  • Plaintext everything — username, password, commands, output.
  • Any sniffer on the path captures the full session.
  • Simple and lightweight — almost no CPU overhead.
  • Fine for lab environments or truly isolated management networks.
  • Completely unacceptable over the internet or untrusted LANs.

SSH (TCP port 22):

  • Full encryption of the session (username, password, commands, output).
  • Server authentication via host key (client can verify it's connecting to the real device).
  • Client authentication via password OR public key.
  • Supports file transfer (SFTP) and port forwarding.
  • Two protocol versions: SSHv1 (insecure — do not use) and SSHv2 (current standard).

Cisco IOS SSH configuration (one-time setup):

hostname R1
ip domain-name example.com                      ! required for key generation
crypto key generate rsa general-keys modulus 2048
username admin privilege 15 secret MyStrongPass
ip ssh version 2
line vty 0 4
 login local
 transport input ssh                             ! restrict to SSH only

The transport input ssh line is critical — without it, the VTY lines accept BOTH Telnet and SSH. Lock them down to SSH only.

Certificate-style auth: SSH public-key authentication — client generates a keypair, puts the public key on the server's authorized_keys file. Stronger than passwords; no re-auth needed once the key is trusted.

Exam hook: 'SSH port?' → TCP 22. 'Telnet port?' → TCP 23. 'Which is encrypted?' → SSH. 'Minimum SSH version for security?' → SSHv2.

hack.memory: SSH = encrypted tunnel (port 22). Telnet = postcard (port 23). Mnemonic: 22 is SAFE (S for SSH starts with 2 letters, safe), 23 is TrashE (T for Telnet). Cisco setup needs: hostname, domain, RSA keys, username, 'transport input ssh' on VTYs. SSHv1 = legacy/insecure; always use SSHv2.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure SSH on a router (hostname, domain, RSA keys, user, 'transport input ssh'). SSH from a PC (telnet client with protocol ssh) and verify password prompt. Try Telnet — rejected because of 'transport input ssh'. Now in Wireshark, capture a Telnet session elsewhere — see plaintext creds; capture SSH — see only encrypted blob.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 41 (SSH). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Guaranteed exam content — know ports, encrypted vs plaintext, 'transport input ssh' configuration.

1.1.g.info:
Three email protocols, each with a specific role and port number.

SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) — TCP port 25 (or 587 with auth): Used for SENDING mail — both from client to server AND between mail servers (server-to-server relay). Plaintext by default; modern deployments wrap in TLS (STARTTLS on 25/587, or implicit TLS on 465).

  • Port 25 — classic SMTP, often blocked by ISPs to prevent spam.
  • Port 587 — Submission port, requires auth. Modern client-to-server default.
  • Port 465 — SMTPS (implicit TLS), legacy-ish but still widely used.

POP3 (Post Office Protocol v3) — TCP port 110 (or 995 for POP3S): Download email from the server to a local client, typically deleting it from the server after download. Good for single-device users; bad for multi-device access (email disappears after first device reads it).

IMAP (Internet Message Access Protocol) — TCP port 143 (or 993 for IMAPS): Access email on the server — messages stay on the server, so any device sees the same inbox. Supports folders and synchronized state. The dominant modern protocol for personal email clients.

Port memorization table:

  • SMTP: 25 (cleartext/STARTTLS), 587 (submission), 465 (SMTPS).
  • POP3: 110 (cleartext), 995 (POP3S, TLS).
  • IMAP: 143 (cleartext), 993 (IMAPS, TLS).

Mail flow: Outlook/Thunderbird client → SMTP (port 587) → your mail server → SMTP (port 25 server-to-server) → recipient's mail server → recipient client pulls via IMAP (993) or POP3 (995).

MX record ties to SMTP: DNS MX records for a domain tell sending servers WHERE to deliver mail. Without MX records, email delivery fails.

Exam hook: 'SMTP port?' → TCP 25 (or 587). 'POP3?' → TCP 110. 'IMAP?' → TCP 143. 'Secure versions?' → 465/995/993 respectively (or use STARTTLS on the base port).

children.name: Send: Client → SMTP (587) → local mail server
Relay: local server → SMTP (25) → recipient's server
Retrieve: Client ← IMAP (143/993) or POP3 (110/995) ← server

hack.memory: Email = the mailbox triangle. SMTP ships it out (25 or 587). POP3 grabs it and deletes (110). IMAP reads it in place (143). Secure versions add ~850 to the number: POP3S=995, IMAPS=993, SMTPS=465. Except SMTP's modern submission port is 587 — memorize this one separately.
hack.practice: Open your email client settings. You'll see either 'IMAP' or 'POP3' for incoming (usually IMAP these days with port 993) and 'SMTP' for outgoing (port 587 for submission). That's the real-world protocol stack. For lab: set up a local Postfix + Dovecot and send mail through it via Telnet on port 25 (raw SMTP session) — very educational for seeing SMTP commands in action.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab covers mail protocols briefly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 13. Port memorization question is near-guaranteed. The 993/995/465/587 secure variants are nice-to-know but often on the exam.

1.1.g.info:
RADIUS (Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service) is the industry-standard AAA protocol defined in RFC 2865. It's an open standard used across vendors, which means Cisco switches can talk to a FreeRADIUS server, a Microsoft NPS, or Cisco ISE — they all speak the same language.

Port numbers:

  • UDP 1812 — Authentication + Authorization (combined).
  • UDP 1813 — Accounting (who logged in, when, how much data).
  • Older legacy ports: UDP 1645 (auth) and UDP 1646 (acct) — rarely used today but still supported.

Key characteristics:

  • UDP-based — lighter than TCP, but no session state or guaranteed delivery.
  • Combines authentication + authorization in one transaction. You can't separate them.
  • Encrypts only the password — the rest of the packet (username, attributes, accounting data) is in cleartext. Weakness vs TACACS+.
  • Shared secret between client and server authenticates the server and encrypts the password.

Use cases:

  • 802.1X wired/wireless network authentication (ISE or NPS as server).
  • VPN authentication (AnyConnect, OpenVPN).
  • Dial-up / remote access (historical).
  • Wi-Fi captive portal backend.

Cisco IOS RADIUS config:

aaa new-model
radius server ISE
 address ipv4 10.0.1.10 auth-port 1812 acct-port 1813
 key MyRadiusSecret
aaa authentication dot1x default group radius
aaa authorization network default group radius
aaa accounting dot1x default start-stop group radius

RADIUS vs TACACS+ (key exam distinction): RADIUS = open standard, UDP, encrypts password only, combines AuthN+AuthZ, typically for NETWORK ACCESS (802.1X). TACACS+ = Cisco proprietary, TCP 49, encrypts entire packet, separates AuthN/AuthZ/Acct, typically for DEVICE ADMIN (CLI access). Next entry dives into TACACS+.

Exam hook: 'RADIUS ports?' → UDP 1812 (auth/authz), 1813 (acct). 'Open standard or proprietary?' → open. 'What does it encrypt?' → password only.

hack.memory: RADIUS = open, UDP, password-only encryption, combines auth + authz. Ports 1812 + 1813. Used for NETWORK ACCESS (who gets on the network). Memory hook: '1812 + 1813, RADIUS loves you and me.' Or 'NPS and ISE both speak RADIUS.'
hack.practice: Set up FreeRADIUS in a VM (it's free and easy). Configure a Cisco switch to use it for 802.1X. Connect a client and authenticate — watch the Access-Request / Access-Accept exchange in Wireshark. Notice the password field is encrypted; everything else is cleartext. This single lab cements RADIUS forever.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. High exam weight — know ports (1812/1813), open standard, UDP, password-only encryption, and contrast with TACACS+.

1.1.g.info:
TACACS+ (Terminal Access Controller Access-Control System Plus) is Cisco's proprietary AAA protocol, typically used for device administrator access (CLI login to switches, routers, firewalls). Uses TCP port 49. While RADIUS handles 'network access' (who gets on the network), TACACS+ handles 'device admin' (who can run commands on the device itself).

Key differentiators vs RADIUS:

  • TCP (port 49) — reliable session-oriented delivery vs RADIUS's UDP.
  • Encrypts the ENTIRE packet body — username, password, attributes, commands — all encrypted. RADIUS only encrypts the password.
  • Separates AuthN / AuthZ / Accounting into three independent processes. You can authenticate against TACACS+ but use local authorization, for example. RADIUS combines AuthN+AuthZ.
  • Cisco-proprietary — though other vendors implement it (Juniper supports TACACS+), it's not a formal open standard like RADIUS.
  • Command authorization — can authorize each CLI command individually. 'User Alice can run show but not configure.' Can't do this with RADIUS.

Typical deployment: Cisco ISE acts as a TACACS+ server (or Cisco ACS historically). Admin SSHes to a switch, switch sends username/password to ISE over TACACS+, ISE responds with auth result + command-authorization ruleset. Every command the admin types is sent to ISE for authorization (if per-command AuthZ is enabled).

Cisco IOS TACACS+ config:

tacacs server ISE
 address ipv4 10.0.1.10
 key MyTacacsSecret
aaa new-model
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local
aaa authorization exec default group tacacs+ local
aaa authorization commands 15 default group tacacs+ local
aaa accounting commands 15 default start-stop group tacacs+

Exam hook: 'TACACS+ port and transport?' → TCP 49. 'What does it encrypt?' → entire packet body. 'Use case?' → device administration. 'Open or proprietary?' → Cisco proprietary. 'Can it authorize commands individually?' → yes.

hack.memory: TACACS+ = Cisco's premium AAA for device admin. TCP 49, full-packet encryption, separate A-A-A processes, per-command authz. Pair it with RADIUS mentally as the two-item comparison: RADIUS for network access (who gets on), TACACS+ for device admin (who configures the box). Memory hook: 'TCP 49 for line 49 commands.'
hack.practice: If you have ISE or tac_plus running: configure a Cisco device to use TACACS+ for login. SSH in — notice the Wireshark packets are fully encrypted (opaque blob). Test per-command auth: configure a 'read-only' profile that allows show commands but not config. Log in as that user and try 'show run' (works) then 'configure terminal' (blocked).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 54. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 27. Guaranteed exam question — pair with RADIUS in the comparison. Know TCP 49 as the standout port number.

1.1.g.info:
Syslog is the standard protocol for sending log messages from network devices to a centralized log server. Uses UDP port 514 by default (syslog over TLS on TCP 6514 is optional). Cisco devices and Linux systems alike use syslog for event logging.

Severity levels (memorize — asked often):

  • 0 — Emergency — system unusable
  • 1 — Alert — immediate action needed
  • 2 — Critical — critical condition
  • 3 — Error — error condition
  • 4 — Warning — warning condition
  • 5 — Notification (Notice) — normal but significant
  • 6 — Informational — routine info
  • 7 — Debugging — debug messages

Mnemonic: 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily' — Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notification, Informational, Debug. Lower number = more severe.

Cisco IOS syslog config:

logging host 10.0.1.20                      ! syslog server IP
logging trap informational                   ! send severity 0-6 to server
logging console warnings                     ! show 0-4 on console
logging buffered 16384 informational          ! store 0-6 in memory buffer
service timestamps log datetime msec
clock timezone CST -6

Syslog message format: *Apr 16 10:32:15.123: %LINK-3-UPDOWN: Interface GigabitEthernet0/1, changed state to up
Breakdown: timestamp | %FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC | description. The 3 between LINK and UPDOWN is the severity level (3 = Error).

Centralized syslog benefits: retention beyond device reboot, correlation across many devices, SIEM integration (Splunk, ELK, QRadar), compliance (log retention requirements).

UDP caveat: Syslog over UDP doesn't guarantee delivery. Logs can be silently lost during heavy traffic or network issues. For critical logging, use TCP syslog or reliable transport.

Exam hook: 'Syslog port?' → UDP 514. 'Severity level for a critical condition?' → 2. 'Lower number = more or less severe?' → more severe.

hack.memory: Syslog = UDP 514, lower number = higher severity. Mnemonic 'Every Awesome Cisco Engineer Will Need Ice-cream Daily' (0-7 Emergency, Alert, Critical, Error, Warning, Notification, Informational, Debug). 'logging trap informational' sends levels 0-6 (since 6 includes everything more severe). Syslog messages: %FACILITY-SEVERITY-MNEMONIC.
hack.practice: Set up a free syslog server (Kiwi, Splunk Free, or rsyslog on Linux). On a Cisco device: 'logging host [server]', 'logging trap informational'. Generate events (shut/no shut an interface) and watch them arrive on the server. Run 'show logging' on the device to see the buffer. Identify the severity code in each message.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40 (Syslog + NTP). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. High exam weight — know port 514, all 8 severity levels, and that lower = more severe.

1.1.g.info:
NTP (Network Time Protocol) synchronizes device clocks across a network. Uses UDP port 123. Time sync is foundational — without it, log timestamps are unreliable (can't correlate events), certificates can't be validated (TLS fails on skew), Kerberos fails (requires <5 min skew), and troubleshooting is hell.

Stratum hierarchy:

  • Stratum 0 — atomic clocks, GPS receivers. Reference sources (not directly queryable over network).
  • Stratum 1 — servers directly synced to Stratum 0. The authoritative public NTP servers.
  • Stratum 2-15 — each stratum syncs from the stratum above. Most enterprise NTP is stratum 2-4.
  • Stratum 16 — unsynchronized (not usable as a time source).

Cisco IOS NTP config:

ntp server pool.ntp.org                     ! client mode — sync from pool
! or to specific server:
ntp server 10.0.1.1 prefer
ntp update-calendar                          ! sync hardware clock from NTP
clock timezone CST -6
clock summer-time CDT recurring

Router as NTP server: A Cisco router can serve NTP to downstream devices. It will advertise its stratum as (upstream server's stratum + 1).

NTP authentication: Prevents clock spoofing attacks.

ntp authenticate
ntp authentication-key 1 md5 MyNtpKey
ntp trusted-key 1
ntp server 10.0.1.1 key 1

Verification: show ntp status (are we synced?), show ntp associations (which servers, which strata, reachability). The '*' next to a server in associations means 'currently selected as sync source.'

Common mistake: configuring NTP but forgetting to set the timezone (clock timezone). NTP always runs in UTC; the timezone translates UTC for display. Wrong timezone = UTC timestamps in logs.

Exam hook: 'NTP port?' → UDP 123. 'What's stratum?' → hierarchy level (lower = closer to authoritative source). 'Why is NTP critical?' → log correlation, certificate validation, Kerberos.

hack.memory: NTP = UDP 123. Stratum = distance from atomic clock. Lower stratum = closer to authoritative source. Stratum 0 is the atomic clock itself; stratum 16 = unsynced. Always pair NTP with 'clock timezone' — NTP is UTC, timezone translates for humans. Without NTP, certs fail and logs lie.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure a router with 'ntp server pool.ntp.org' (or any public NTP). Run 'show ntp status' — after a few minutes you should see 'Clock is synchronized, stratum N'. Add another device pointing at this router as NTP server. Observe it picks up stratum N+1. Bonus: simulate auth with 'ntp authentication-key'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. Guaranteed exam port question (UDP 123). Know stratum concept. Also know why NTP matters: log correlation, cert validation.

1.1.g.info:
SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is the standard for monitoring and managing network devices. An SNMP manager (NMS — e.g., SolarWinds, PRTG, LibreNMS) polls devices periodically to gather interface stats, CPU/memory, uptime, etc. Devices can also push asynchronous events to the NMS. Uses UDP port 161 (queries/polls) and UDP port 162 (traps/notifications).

SNMP versions:

  • SNMPv1 (1988) — original. Community strings in plaintext. No encryption. Obsolete.
  • SNMPv2c (1993) — added bulk operations (GETBULK), better error codes. Still plaintext community strings. Widely deployed.
  • SNMPv3 (1998) — added authentication (MD5/SHA) and encryption (DES/AES). User-based security model (USM) with three levels: noAuthNoPriv, authNoPriv, authPriv. The ONLY version to use on modern networks.

Key operations:

  • GET — retrieve a specific value (OID).
  • GETNEXT / GETBULK — retrieve next-in-order or bulk values.
  • SET — write a value (e.g., change an interface description).
  • TRAP — device-initiated event notification to NMS.
  • INFORM — trap variant with acknowledgment (reliable delivery).

MIB + OID: Every SNMP variable lives at an OID (Object Identifier) — a dotted-number hierarchy like 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.3.0 (sysUpTime). The MIB (Management Information Base) is the schema describing what OIDs exist and what they mean.

Cisco IOS SNMPv3 config:

snmp-server group ADMIN-GROUP v3 priv
snmp-server user admin ADMIN-GROUP v3 auth sha MyAuthPass priv aes 128 MyPrivPass
snmp-server host 10.0.1.20 traps version 3 priv admin
snmp-server enable traps
snmp-server contact "netops@example.com"
snmp-server location "Main Office Rack 3"

Read-only vs read-write: Polling (GET) is safe. SET (read-write) is dangerous — an attacker with read-write community string or credentials can modify configs remotely. Avoid read-write SNMP unless absolutely required.

Exam hook: 'SNMP ports?' → UDP 161 poll, 162 traps. 'Which version has encryption?' → v3 only. 'What's an OID?' → identifier for a managed object.

right.label: SNMPv3
items: Three sec levels: noAuth, auth, priv

hack.memory: SNMP = 161 poll in (one-six-one, 'One queries In'), 162 trap out (one-six-two, 'Two traps out'). Versions: v1/v2c = plaintext community strings (bad); v3 = auth + encryption (good — use this). OID = the address of a variable in the MIB. SET commands = dangerous, avoid read-write unless essential.
hack.practice: On a Cisco device: 'snmp-server community public RO' (v2c, for lab only). From a Linux box: 'snmpwalk -v2c -c public [router-ip]'. See the whole MIB dump.
