Now configure v3: create a user with auth/priv, and 'snmpwalk -v3 -l authPriv -u admin -a SHA -A authpass -x AES -X privpass [router-ip]'. v3 is more typing but far more secure.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 40. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 9. High exam weight — know ports, v3 as the secure version, MIB/OID concept, trap vs inform.

1.1.h.info:
PSE (Power Sourcing Equipment) is the device that provides electrical power over Ethernet cabling. In practice, the PSE is almost always a PoE switch, but it can also be a dedicated midspan injector — a standalone device that inserts PoE into an Ethernet link between a non-PoE switch and a powered device.

How PSEs decide whether to supply power:

  1. Port comes up with a PoE-capable cable.
  2. PSE sends a low-voltage detection probe (~2.7V).
  3. PSE measures the resistance. A valid PD presents ~25kΩ; non-PoE devices (regular PCs, dumb hubs) present different resistance and are skipped.
  4. Once detection passes, PSE applies classification — a brief current pulse that tells the PSE the PD's maximum power class (0-8).
  5. PSE allocates the corresponding wattage from its power budget and turns on full voltage (48V DC typical).

PSE types:

  • Endspan — PoE is built into the switch. Most common in modern enterprise deployments. Example: Catalyst 9300-48P (48-port PoE+).
  • Midspan — separate injector sits between a non-PoE switch and the PD. Useful for retrofitting older switches without replacement. Cheaper for low-port-count upgrades.

Power budgets matter: A PSE switch has a total wattage budget across all PoE ports. Exceeding the budget means the switch denies power to lower-priority ports. Plan power budget BEFORE you fill the switch with high-wattage devices.

Exam hook: 'What does PSE stand for?' → Power Sourcing Equipment. 'Examples?' → PoE switch, midspan injector. 'How does the PSE detect a valid PD?' → resistance check (25kΩ signature).

hack.memory: PSE = 'Provides Source Energy.' Whatever's giving power is the PSE. Switch with PoE = endspan PSE. Standalone injector = midspan PSE. The PSE is the outlet; the PD is the appliance. Memory: P-S-E sounds like 'pays' — the PSE pays the electric bill.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: connect a PoE switch to an IP phone. Check 'show power inline' — the phone is drawing wattage, switch is allocating. Remove the PoE switch and replace with a non-PoE switch + midspan injector (not modeled in PT but conceptually: phone still works). Real lab: use a Cisco 9300-48P and plug phones/APs — 'show power inline' gives full budget overview.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25 (PoE). Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: know PSE = power provider, typically a switch or midspan injector. Pair with PD for the two-item comparison.

1.1.h.info:
PD (Powered Device) is the device drawing power from a PoE link. Common PDs in enterprise networks: IP phones, wireless access points, IP cameras, digital signage, wireless bridges, door access readers, sometimes thin clients.

What makes something a PoE PD:

  1. Presents a ~25kΩ resistance signature that the PSE detects.
  2. Supports the classification pulse to announce its max power class.
  3. Accepts power on specific pin pairs (2 pairs for 802.3af/at; all 4 pairs for 802.3bt).

Common PDs and their typical power class:

  • IP phone (Cisco 7841, 8851) — 6-7W (Class 2, fits 802.3af).
  • Entry-level AP (Cisco 9105) — 12W (Class 3, 802.3af).
  • Modern AP (Cisco 9120/9130) — 25W (Class 4, 802.3at required).
  • PTZ IP camera — 30-60W (802.3bt Type 3 or higher).
  • Wi-Fi 6E AP with multi-radio — 40-60W (802.3bt Type 3).
  • Video conference endpoint (Cisco Webex desk) — 60-90W (802.3bt Type 4).

PD behavior under insufficient power: If the PSE allocates less than the PD needs (budget constrained, wrong standard), the PD may:

  • Boot in reduced-capability mode (dual-band AP runs one radio only).
  • Fail to boot at all (loops through reset).
  • Boot and crash intermittently under load.

This is why matching PSE standard to PD requirement is critical. A 25W AP plugged into a 15.4W 802.3af port will misbehave.

LLDP-MED power negotiation: PDs and PSEs can use LLDP-MED TLV extensions to negotiate exact power requirements beyond the coarse class-based allocation. More efficient power budgeting — the AP tells the switch 'I actually only need 18W, not the full 30W Class 4' and frees 12W for other ports.

Exam hook: 'What does PD stand for?' → Powered Device. 'Examples?' → IP phone, AP, IP camera. 'What standard does a 25W AP need?' → 802.3at (PoE+).

hack.memory: PD = 'Pulls Direct-current.' The appliance end of PoE. IP phones, APs, cameras, digital signage. Mnemonic: P-D = 'Power Drinker.' Match the PD's wattage need to the PSE's standard — af=15W, at=30W, bt=60/90W.
hack.practice: Look up the Cisco datasheet for any AP or IP phone you encounter. Find its 'power requirement' or 'PoE class' — notice the spec sheet clearly states '802.3at required' or similar. Make a flashcard matching common PD types to PoE standards. Knowing 'Cisco 9130 AP needs PoE+' is the kind of specific fact the exam may test.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: know PD = powered device, and common examples. Pair with PSE.

1.1.h.info:
IEEE 802.3af was the first PoE standard, ratified in 2003. It delivers up to 15.4W at the PSE, with the PD actually receiving ~12.95W after cable resistive losses. This is enough for basic IP phones and entry-level APs, but insufficient for modern high-performance wireless.

Technical details:

  • Max power at PSE port: 15.4W.
  • Guaranteed power at PD: 12.95W.
  • Voltage: 44-57V DC (typically ~48V).
  • Max current: 350mA.
  • Uses 2 of 4 twisted pairs in an Ethernet cable.
  • Two 'modes' of power delivery:
    • Mode A — power on pairs 1/2 and 3/6 (data pairs for 10/100BASE-T).
    • Mode B — power on pairs 4/5 and 7/8 (spare pairs).
  • PSE and PD negotiate which mode to use.

PD classes under 802.3af:

  • Class 0 — 0.44-12.95W (default, no classification).
  • Class 1 — 0.44-3.84W (very low power).
  • Class 2 — 3.84-6.49W (IP phones).
  • Class 3 — 6.49-12.95W (basic APs).

Cable requirements: Cat5 or better. Cat5e and Cat6 are fully supported.

Use cases (today): IP phones (Cisco 7841, 8851), basic APs (Aironet 1140), door access readers, VoIP speakers. Any device that needs 12W or less.

Marketing name: 'PoE' — when you see 'PoE' without a '+' or '++', it usually means 802.3af.

Exam hook: '802.3af max wattage at PSE?' → 15.4W. 'At PD?' → 12.95W. 'How many cable pairs used?' → 2 of 4.

children.name: Use: basic IP phones, entry APs

hack.memory: 802.3af = 'f' for 'fifteen' (15.4W). The baseline PoE. Two pairs of wire, 12.95W delivered. Fine for an IP phone or simple AP. Anything more demanding needs PoE+ (at) or PoE++ (bt).
hack.practice: On a real PoE switch: connect an IP phone (Class 2) to a port. Run 'show power inline int Gi0/1'. You'll see Admin State=auto, Oper State=on, Power=~7W, Class=2, Standard=IEEE 802.3af. This is the baseline PoE experience.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: near-guaranteed 'af = 15.4W' memorization question. Remember the mnemonic: af=fifteen, at=thirty, bt=big.

1.1.h.info:
IEEE 802.3at, ratified in 2009, is the successor to 802.3af. Delivers up to 30W at the PSE with ~25.5W guaranteed at the PD. Marketed as PoE+. The doubled power enables modern 802.11n/ac dual-radio APs, pan-tilt IP cameras, and higher-end IP phones with video screens.

Technical details:

  • Max power at PSE port: 30W.
  • Guaranteed power at PD: 25.5W.
  • Voltage: 50-57V DC.
  • Max current: 600mA (nearly double af's 350mA).
  • Still uses 2 of 4 twisted pairs (same as af).
  • Introduces Class 4 (12.95-25.5W).
  • Backward compatible — 802.3at PSE can still power 802.3af PDs.

LLDP-MED required for 30W: To negotiate full 30W delivery, the PSE and PD typically use LLDP-MED TLVs. Without LLDP, the PD falls back to af-level classification (~13W). This is why some APs 'boot but one radio disabled' when plugged into a PoE+ switch but LLDP is misconfigured.

Cable requirements: Cat5e minimum due to higher current (higher I²R losses).

Use cases: Cisco APs like 9120, 9130, 2800/3800 series. Pan-tilt IP cameras. Higher-end IP phones with displays and video. Some thin clients and digital signage.

Exam hook: '802.3at max wattage at PSE?' → 30W. 'At PD?' → 25.5W. 'Cable minimum?' → Cat5e. 'Marketing name?' → PoE+. 'Backward compatible with af?' → yes.

hack.memory: 802.3at = 't' for 'thirty' (30W). Doubles af's power on the same 2 pairs by doubling the current. PoE+ with a plus sign. Needed for any modern AP. LLDP-MED handshake required for full 30W — otherwise you drop back to af levels.
hack.practice: On a Catalyst switch with PoE+ ports: connect a Cisco 9130 AP. Run 'show power inline' — notice Class=4 and Power=~30W. Disable LLDP-MED and watch the AP drop to ~15W (class 3 fallback). Real-world: this is THE most common 'my AP isn't booting fully' support ticket.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: near-guaranteed 'at = 30W' question. Know it's PoE+, 2 pairs, 600mA, Class 4.

1.1.h.info:
IEEE 802.3bt Type 3, ratified in 2018, is the first tier of the 802.3bt standard. Delivers up to 60W at the PSE with ~51W at the PD. Marketed (with Type 4) as PoE++ or 4PPoE (4-pair PoE). Key innovation: uses all 4 twisted pairs to deliver more current without exceeding per-pair limits.

Technical details:

  • Max power at PSE port: 60W.
  • Guaranteed at PD: 51W.
  • Voltage: 50-57V DC.
  • Max current: 600mA per pair, ~1.2A total across all 4 pairs.
  • Uses all 4 cable pairs (versus 2 for af/at).
  • Introduces Classes 5-6.
  • Backward compatible with af/at PDs.

Cable requirements: Cat5e minimum, Cat6 recommended for safety margin. The higher current stresses cable resistance; better cable = less voltage drop and less heat.

Use cases:

  • Wi-Fi 6 / 6E APs with multiple radios (Cisco 9136, 9166).
  • Digital signage displays.
  • Outdoor PTZ cameras with heaters.
  • Video conferencing endpoints.
  • Some POS terminals.

Heat concern: 60W flowing through a bundle of Ethernet cables generates measurable heat. Large PoE deployments can add a few degrees to cable-bundle temperature — a consideration in telecom closet thermal planning.

Exam hook: '802.3bt Type 3 max wattage?' → 60W (PSE) / 51W (PD). 'How many pairs used?' → all 4. 'Marketing name?' → PoE++ or 4PPoE.

children.name: Use: Wi-Fi 6 APs, signage, PTZ cams

hack.memory: 802.3bt = 'b' for 'big' — Type 3 is 60W, Type 4 is 90W. Key shift: uses ALL 4 pairs (af/at only used 2). Why: more pairs = more current path = more total wattage without burning any single pair. Needed for Wi-Fi 6 APs and PTZ cameras.
hack.practice: On a Catalyst 9300-48UXM (supports 802.3bt): connect a Wi-Fi 6 AP (9136). Run 'show power inline'. Notice the IEEE Standard field = 'IEEE 802.3bt Type 3', Class=6, Power ~45W. Compare to a Catalyst 9200 (only supports PoE+/at) — same AP would boot in reduced mode.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: know 'bt Type 3 = 60W using all 4 pairs'. This is newer content — make sure your study material is current.

1.1.h.info:
IEEE 802.3bt Type 4 is the top tier of PoE. Delivers up to 90W at the PSE, ~71W at the PD. Same 802.3bt standard as Type 3 but higher current per pair. Enables some of the most power-hungry PoE use cases: PTZ cameras with built-in heaters, large displays, premium video endpoints, and low-power laptops.

Technical details:

  • Max power at PSE port: 90W.
  • Guaranteed at PD: 71W.
  • Voltage: 52-57V DC.
  • Max current: 960mA per pair (nearly double Type 3).
  • Uses all 4 twisted pairs.
  • Introduces Classes 7-8.

Cable requirements: Cat6A recommended. Cat5e will work but at higher temperatures and with more voltage drop. At 90W, cable quality matters materially.

Use cases:

  • High-end PTZ cameras with environmental heating (for outdoor winter deployments).
  • 60-70W video conferencing endpoints (Cisco Webex Desk Pro).
  • Large digital signage displays (up to ~55 inches).
  • Low-power laptops with PoE-powered USB-C docks.
  • Industrial / IIoT devices with high power draw.

Type 4 vs Cisco UPoE+: Cisco's proprietary UPoE+ (also 90W) predated 802.3bt Type 4 and is largely compatible. The industry has standardized on 802.3bt Type 4 as the open standard.

Budget impact: A 48-port switch fully populated with 90W PDs would need 4,320W of PoE budget — far exceeding any single switch's capacity. Large Type 4 deployments are typically mixed (some Type 4, some lower) with careful budget planning.

Exam hook: '802.3bt Type 4 max wattage?' → 90W (PSE) / 71W (PD). 'Cable recommendation?' → Cat6A. 'All 4 pairs?' → yes.

hack.memory: 802.3bt Type 4 = 'b-t' for 'big-time' (90W). Top tier of PoE. All 4 pairs at max current. Cat6A for safety. Use cases = the weird big-watt stuff: video endpoints, heated outdoor cams, PoE laptops. Rare in generic deployments, common in specific vertical markets.
hack.practice: On a Catalyst 9300-24UXM or 9600 (supports 802.3bt Type 4): connect a Webex Desk Pro or similar. 'show power inline' → Class=8, Power ~70W, Standard='IEEE 802.3bt Type 4'. Calculate budget: 24 Type 4 ports × 90W = 2160W; check if the switch's PoE budget supports it.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Exam: know 'bt Type 4 = 90W'. The af/at/bt progression (15/30/60-90) is heavily tested.

1.1.h.info:
UPoE (Universal Power over Ethernet) is Cisco's proprietary PoE extension that predated 802.3bt. It delivers 60W at the PSE using all 4 pairs — roughly equivalent to 802.3bt Type 3. UPoE+ extends to 90W, matching Type 4.

Why UPoE existed: 802.3bt wasn't ratified until 2018. Cisco needed to ship high-wattage PoE earlier (UPoE launched ~2011) to support evolving PoE needs — high-end Cisco IP phones, 60W thin clients, large digital signage. UPoE filled the gap.

Compatibility:

  • UPoE PDs work on UPoE PSEs (Cisco-to-Cisco deployments).
  • UPoE is generally backward-compatible with 802.3af/at PDs.
  • Modern Cisco switches (9300, 9400, 9500) typically support both UPoE and 802.3bt.
  • A UPoE-only PD plugged into a non-Cisco 802.3bt switch may or may not negotiate correctly — vendor interop isn't guaranteed.

Current guidance: For new deployments, use 802.3bt (Type 3 or Type 4) as the open standard. UPoE exists for installed-base compatibility but new specs should follow IEEE.

Exam relevance: Low. You may see UPoE mentioned in CCNA material as a Cisco-proprietary 60W predecessor to 802.3bt. No deep memorization needed.

hack.memory: UPoE = Cisco's early version of 'bt', before IEEE finalized. 60W (UPoE) and 90W (UPoE+). Basically overlaps with 802.3bt Type 3 and Type 4. For new deployments use IEEE bt; UPoE is legacy.
hack.practice: Flashcard: 'Cisco's proprietary 60W PoE that predates 802.3bt Type 3?' → UPoE. 'Cisco's 90W equivalent?' → UPoE+. No hands-on lab needed.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25 mentions UPoE briefly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Low exam weight — name recognition only.

1.1.h.info:
Before a PSE turns on full 48V power, it performs two safety checks — detection and classification — to make sure the connected device is actually a valid PD that wants PoE. This prevents sending voltage into a non-PoE device (which could damage it) and lets the PSE allocate the right amount of power.

Detection phase:

  1. PSE sends a low-voltage probe (~2.7-10V) onto the port's pairs.
  2. Measures current draw to calculate resistance.
  3. Valid PoE PD presents ~25kΩ (IEEE-specified signature resistor).
  4. If no signature → PSE does not apply power. Safe for non-PoE devices.
  5. If signature valid → proceed to classification.

Classification phase:

  1. PSE briefly applies ~15V to the port.
  2. The PD responds with a current signature indicating its class (0-8).
  3. PSE allocates power based on the class:
    • Class 0 — default, up to 12.95W (no explicit class).
    • Class 1 — up to 3.84W.
    • Class 2 — up to 6.49W.
    • Class 3 — up to 12.95W (IP phones, basic APs).
    • Class 4 — up to 25.5W (PoE+ required; modern APs).
    • Class 5 — up to 40W (802.3bt Type 3).
    • Class 6 — up to 51W (802.3bt Type 3).
    • Class 7 — up to 62W (802.3bt Type 4).
    • Class 8 — up to 71W (802.3bt Type 4).

LLDP-MED fine-tuning: After classification, the PD can negotiate a more precise power budget using LLDP-MED TLVs. This lets the switch allocate less than the coarse class allows, freeing budget for other ports. Essential for efficient use of the switch's total PoE budget.

Exam hook: 'What is the PSE detection signature resistance?' → ~25kΩ. 'How many PoE classes?' → 9 (0-8). 'What protocol fine-tunes allocation beyond class?' → LLDP-MED.

hack.memory: Detection = 'is this even a PoE device?' (resistance check, 25kΩ). Classification = 'how much does it need?' (current-pulse class 0-8). Then optional LLDP-MED = 'can we be more precise?' (saves budget). Without detection, PSEs would fry non-PoE devices.
hack.practice: On a PoE switch: plug a regular PC (non-PoE) into a PoE port. 'show power inline' shows 'off / No PoE device'. Now plug in a phone. Same command shows the class, allocated wattage, and consumed wattage. The switch skipped the PC via failed detection, then powered the phone via passed detection + classification.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. Medium exam weight — know detection (25kΩ) and classification (0-8 classes). LLDP-MED is bonus.

1.1.h.info:
A PoE power budget is the total wattage a switch can supply across all its PoE ports simultaneously. Every PoE switch has a finite budget — based on its internal power supplies — and exceeding that budget means some ports will be denied power. Proper budget planning is critical for any PoE deployment.

Example budgets (real Cisco data):

  • Catalyst 9200-48P — 740W PoE budget across 48 ports.
  • Catalyst 9300-48P — 505W single PS, 1110W with dual PS.
  • Catalyst 9300-48UXM (UPoE) — 945W or 1900W with dual PS.
  • Catalyst 9300-48H (802.3bt) — up to ~1440W with high-capacity PS.

The math: 48 ports × 30W (PoE+ max) = 1440W. A 740W-budget switch can power at most ~24 ports at full PoE+. If every port needs 30W, budget = oversubscribed. The switch handles this two ways: (1) some ports get less than requested (if LLDP-MED allows step-down), or (2) some ports get no power at all (based on priority).

Priority configuration: Control which ports get power first when budget is tight.

interface Gi0/1
 power inline port priority critical           ! top priority (APs, phones)
interface Gi0/2
 power inline port priority high
interface Gi0/3
 power inline port priority low                ! lowest — first to lose power

When budget is exhausted, low-priority ports are denied first. Critical ports always get power if possible.

Per-port power control:

  • power inline auto — default, detect + classify + allocate.
  • power inline auto max 15400 — cap at 15.4W (simulate af on an at port).
  • power inline static max 30000 — reserve 30W regardless of actual consumption (defensive allocation).
  • power inline never — disable PoE on this port entirely.

Dual power supplies: Many PoE switches support redundant/combined PS. Two 1100W PSs can either run in redundant mode (one backs up the other → budget = 1100W) or combined mode (budget = 2200W, but no redundancy). Choose based on reliability needs.

Exam hook: 'What happens when PoE budget is exceeded?' → lower-priority ports denied power. 'How do you configure port priority?' → 'power inline port priority [low|high|critical]'. 'How to disable PoE on a port?' → 'power inline never'.

hack.memory: PoE budget = the switch's electrical paycheck. Sum of all PD wattage must stay under the paycheck. Priority decides who eats first when money runs out: critical > high > low. Commands: 'power inline port priority [critical/high/low]' and 'power inline never' to disable.
hack.practice: On a PoE switch: 'show power inline' — top shows total budget and consumed wattage. Plug in enough PDs to approach the budget. Assign some as critical, some as low priority. Unplug a power supply (if dual PS) and watch low-priority ports drop while critical stays up. This is exactly how the exam frames the question.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 25. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 11. High exam weight — budget math + priority + 'power inline' commands all fair game.

1.1.h.info:
Two different PSE architectures for delivering PoE to PDs. The difference is where the PoE is injected into the cable.

Endspan: PoE is integrated directly into the switch. Every port on the switch is a PSE. This is by far the most common PoE deployment in modern networks.

  • Pros: one device, simple cabling, centralized management, single power supply, fewer failure points.
  • Cons: all-or-nothing — you have to buy a PoE switch upfront, even if only a few ports need PoE.
  • Examples: Cisco Catalyst 9200-48P, 9300-48UXM, 2960-48PST-L.

Midspan: A dedicated inline injector sits between a non-PoE switch and a PD. The switch sends regular Ethernet (no power) to the injector; the injector adds PoE and forwards to the PD. Two cables, essentially: switch-to-injector and injector-to-PD.

  • Pros: lets you add PoE to existing non-PoE switches without replacement, cheaper for small deployments (1-2 APs).
  • Cons: extra device per port, more cables, harder to centralize management.
  • Examples: Cisco AIR-PWRINJ6 single-port injector for APs, Phihong 8/16/24-port midspan hubs.

When to use each:

  • New build with 10+ PoE devices → endspan PoE switch. Simpler at scale.
  • Adding one AP to an existing non-PoE LAN → midspan injector. Cheap and quick.
  • Mixed facility with some PoE needs → consider both: endspan for the majority, midspans for isolated use cases.

Power capacity difference: Most single-port midspan injectors support up to 802.3at (30W). For 802.3bt power, midspan options are more limited and expensive — endspan 9300-class switches dominate.

Exam hook: 'What is an endspan PSE?' → a PoE switch. 'Midspan?' → a standalone injector between a non-PoE switch and a PD. 'Which is more common in modern deployments?' → endspan.

hack.memory: Endspan = PoE is at the END of the line (the switch itself is the PSE). Midspan = PoE is in the MIDDLE (an injector between switch and PD). Modern = endspan. Legacy upgrade = midspan. Memory: 'ENDspan = your SWITCH ends with P' (PoE built in).
hack.practice: Look at any office Ethernet closet: do you see PoE switches (endspan) or separate injector bricks between switches and end devices (midspan)? Probably endspan in an enterprise, possibly midspan in a small office. Make a flashcard contrasting the two with a cable diagram for each.

1.1.h.info:
show power inline is the primary Cisco IOS command for inspecting PoE status on a switch. It's the first command any network engineer runs when troubleshooting 'why isn't this phone/AP getting power?'

Summary output (without interface specifier):

Module   Available     Used     Remaining
                (Watts)     (Watts)     (Watts)
------   ---------     ----     ---------
1         370.0          87.5     282.5

Interface  Admin  Oper       Power   Device              Class Max
-----------------------------------------------------------------
Gi1/0/1    auto   on         15.4     IP Phone 8851      3     15.4
Gi1/0/2    auto   on         30.0     AIR-AP9130I        4     30.0
Gi1/0/3    auto   off        0.0      n/a                n/a   30.0
Gi1/0/4    auto   on         30.0     AIR-AP9130I        4     30.0
Gi1/0/5    never  off        0.0      n/a                n/a   0.0

Read the output:

  • Available / Used / Remaining — total PoE budget and utilization across the whole switch/stack.
  • Admin — auto (default), static, or never. 'never' = PoE disabled on that port.
  • Oper — on (delivering power), off (no PD detected or admin=never), err-disable (fault condition).
  • Power — actual wattage being delivered right now.
  • Device — the PD's reported name (via CDP/LLDP).
  • Class — PoE class (0-8) detected during classification.
  • Max — maximum wattage this port is allowed to provide.

Per-interface detail: show power inline Gi1/0/2 gives more detail, including which IEEE standard is active (af / at / bt Type 3 / bt Type 4) and the PSE-PD negotiation history.

Troubleshooting workflow:

  1. show power inline → check port Admin + Oper state.
  2. If Admin='never' → enable with power inline auto.
  3. If Oper='off' + Admin='auto' → detection failed. Check cable, PD compatibility.
  4. If Oper='err-disable' → port faulted (overcurrent?). shutdown/no shutdown or investigate PD.
  5. If 'insufficient power' → over-budget, check priority + total budget.

Other related commands: show power inline police (policing stats), show power inline consumption (actual consumption per port).

Exam hook: 'What command shows PoE status per port?' → show power inline. 'What field tells you the PD's PoE class?' → Class column. 'How to disable PoE on a port?' → 'power inline never'.

hack.memory: 'show power inline' = PoE X-ray. First line: total budget summary (Available/Used/Remaining). Then table: per-port Admin, Oper, Power, Device, Class, Max. Memorize the columns — they're literally the first thing on the exam's screenshot question.
hack.practice: On any PoE switch: 'show power inline' with various devices connected. Identify each column. Then 'show power inline Gi1/0/1' for detail. Try 'show power inline priority' and 'show power inline consumption'. These commands are rare but fair game on the exam.
